examples.title: Pseudocode: Oozie Workflow Execution Logic
examples.description: Demonstrates the control flow logic of an Oozie workflow with decision nodes, parallel execution, and error handling
examples.code:
ALGORITHM OozieWorkflowExecution
INPUT: workflow definition, job properties
OUTPUT: workflow completion status

BEGIN
    // Workflow starts here
    workflow_start()
    
    // Step 1: Data Ingestion (MapReduce)
    SET action = "data-ingestion"
    EXECUTE MapReduce job
    
    IF job_status == SUCCESS THEN
        TRANSITION to "data-cleaning"
    ELSE
        TRANSITION to "kill-node"
        LOG error message
        EXIT workflow
    END IF
    
    // Step 2: Fork - parallel processing
    FORK parallel_tasks
        BRANCH 1: Execute temperature analysis
        BRANCH 2: Execute humidity analysis
        BRANCH 3: Execute pressure analysis
    END FORK
    
    // Wait for all parallel branches to complete
    JOIN parallel_tasks
    
    // Step 3: Decision node - check data quality
    DECISION check_data_quality
        IF data_quality_score > 0.9 THEN
            TO high_quality_branch
        ELSE IF data_quality_score < 0.5 THEN
            TO low_quality_branch
        ELSE
            TO normal_processing
        END IF
    END DECISION
    
    // Step 4: Data Analysis based on decision
    SWITCH selected_branch
        CASE high_quality_branch:
            EXECUTE advanced_analytics()
        CASE low_quality_branch:
            EXECUTE basic_filtering()
        DEFAULT:
            EXECUTE standard_analytics()
    END SWITCH
    
    // Step 5: Generate Report
    EXECUTE shell script "generate-report.sh"
    
    // Step 6: Export to database
    EXECUTE Sqoop export
    
    // Workflow completed successfully
    workflow_end()
    
EXCEPTION HANDLER:
    RETRY action up to 3 times with 5 minute interval
    IF all retries failed THEN
        TRANSITION to "fail" node
        SEND notification email
    END IF

END ALGORITHM
examples.explanation: This pseudocode shows the flow of an Oozie workflow including: start node, action execution with success/failure transitions, fork/join for parallel processing, decision nodes for conditional branching, and error handling with retry logic. The workflow represents a typical IoT data processing pipeline.

examples.title: Python: Oozie Workflow Submission and Monitoring
examples.description: Demonstrates how to submit, monitor, and manage Oozie workflows using Python
examples.code:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Oozie Workflow Client - Submit and Monitor Workflows
This module demonstrates interacting with Oozie REST API
"""

import requests
from datetime import datetime
import time
import xml.etree.ElementTree as ET

class OozieClient:
    """Client for interacting with Oozie REST API"""
    
    def __init__(self, oozie_url, user='hadoop'):
        self.oozie_url = oozie_url.rstrip('/')
        self.user = user
        self.headers = {'Content-Type': 'application/xml'}
    
    def submit_workflow(self, workflow_xml, properties):
        """
        Submit an Oozie workflow job
        """
        # Combine properties into key=value format
        props_str = '\n'.join([f"{k}={v}" for k, v in properties.items()])
        
        # Prepare multipart request
        files = {
            'workflow.xml': ('workflow.xml', workflow_xml, 'application/xml'),
            'properties': ('job.properties', props_str, 'text/plain')
        }
        
        url = f"{self.oozie_url}/versions/1/jobs"
        response = requests.post(url, files=files)
        
        if response.status_code == 200:
            job_id = response.json().get('id')
            print(f"Workflow submitted successfully: {job_id}")
            return job_id
        else:
            print(f"Error submitting workflow: {response.text}")
            return None
    
    def get_job_status(self, job_id):
        """
        Get the status of a workflow job
        """
        url = f"{self.oozie_url}/versions/1/job/{job_id}?show=info"
        response = requests.get(url)
        
        if response.status_code == 200:
            return response.json()
        return None
    
    def monitor_workflow(self, job_id, poll_interval=30):
        """
        Monitor workflow until completion
        """
        terminal_states = ['SUCCEEDED', 'FAILED', 'KILLED']
        
        while True:
            status = self.get_job_status(job_id)
            if status:
                job_status = status.get('status')
                print(f"[{datetime.now()}] Job {job_id}: {job_status}")
                
                if job_status in terminal_states:
                    if job_status == 'SUCCEEDED':
                        print("Workflow completed successfully!")
                    else:
                        print(f"Workflow {job_status.lower()}")
                    return job_status
            
            time.sleep(poll_interval)
    
    def rerun_workflow(self, job_id, skip_nodes=None):
        """
        Rerun a workflow, optionally skipping certain nodes
        """
        url = f"{self.oozie_url}/versions/1/job/{job_id}"
        
        payload = {'action': 'start'}
        if skip_nodes:
            payload['skipNodes'] = ','.join(skip_nodes)
        
        response = requests.put(url, json=payload)
        return response.status_code == 200
    
    def kill_workflow(self, job_id):
        """
        Kill a running workflow
        """
        url = f"{self.oozie_url}/versions/1/job/{job_id}"
        response = requests.put(url, json={'action': 'kill'})
        return response.status_code == 200


def create_sensor_workflow_xml():
    """Generate Oozie workflow XML for sensor data processing"""
    return '''<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<workflow-app name="sensor-processing-wf" 
              xmlns="uri:oozie:workflow:0.5">
    <start to="data-ingestion"/>
    
    <action name="data-ingestion">
        <map-reduce>
            <job-tracker>${jobTracker}</job-tracker>
            <name-node>${nameNode}</name-node>
            <configuration>
                <property>
                    <name>mapred.input.dir</name>
                    <value>${inputDir}</value>
                </property>
                <property>
                    <name>mapred.output.dir</name>
                    <value>${outputDir}/raw</value>
                </property>
            </configuration>
        </map-reduce>
        <ok to="data-cleaning"/>
        <error to="fail"/>
    </action>
    
    <action name="data-cleaning">
        <pig>
            <job-tracker>${jobTracker}</job-tracker>
            <name-node>${nameNode}</name-node>
            <script>clean-data.pig</script>
            <param>INPUT=${outputDir}/raw</param>
            <param>OUTPUT=${outputDir}/clean</param>
        </pig>
        <ok to="data-analysis"/>
        <error to="fail"/>
    </action>
    
    <action name="data-analysis">
        <hive>
            <job-tracker>${jobTracker}</job-tracker>
            <name-node>${nameNode}</name-node>
            <script>analyze.hql</script>
        </hive>
        <ok to="end"/>
        <error to="fail"/>
    </action>
    
    <kill name="fail">
        <message>Workflow failed: ${wf:errorMessage()}</message>
    </kill>
    
    <end name="end"/>
</workflow-app>'''


# Example usage
if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Initialize Oozie client
    client = OozieClient("http://localhost:11000/oozie")
    
    # Create workflow XML
    workflow_xml = create_sensor_workflow_xml()
    
    # Define job properties
    properties = {
        'jobTracker': 'localhost:8032',
        'nameNode': 'hdfs://localhost:9000',
        'inputDir': '/data/sensors/raw',
        'outputDir': '/data/sensors/processed',
        'user.name': 'hadoop'
    }
    
    # Submit and monitor workflow
    job_id = client.submit_workflow(workflow_xml, properties)
    
    if job_id:
        client.monitor_workflow(job_id)
examples.explanation: This Python implementation demonstrates how to interact with Apache Oozie using its REST API. It includes a client class for submitting workflows, monitoring job status, and managing workflow lifecycle. The example shows a complete sensor data processing pipeline submission similar to the IoT use case described in the topic.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is the number of polling iterations

examples.title: Java: Oozie Coordinator Job Scheduler
examples.description: Demonstrates how to create and schedule Oozie coordinator jobs for time-based workflow execution
examples.explanation: This Java implementation demonstrates how to programmatically generate Oozie Coordinator and Bundle XML configurations. It shows the three-tier hierarchy of Oozie: Workflows (individual jobs), Coordinators (time-based scheduling), and Bundles (grouping multiple coordinators). The example creates scheduling for IoT sensor data processing with hourly and daily frequencies.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is the number of coordinators in the bundle
